evals.name: onboarding-redesign
evals.prompt: Redesign an onboarding flow for a project management app — 4 steps: welcome, create first project, invite team, set up first task. Build it as a working HTML page with animations.
evals.expected_output: A polished, multi-step onboarding flow with clear progress indicator, smooth transitions between steps, helpful microcopy, and responsive design

assertions.name: has-4-steps
assertions.description: All 4 onboarding steps are present

assertions.name: has-progress-indicator
assertions.description: Visual progress bar or step indicator

assertions.name: has-step-transitions
assertions.description: Animated transitions between steps

assertions.name: responsive-layout
assertions.description: Uses responsive CSS

assertions.name: has-microcopy
assertions.description: Helpful labels, descriptions, and CTAs

assertions.name: accessible-markup
assertions.description: Uses semantic HTML elements

evals.name: pricing-table
evals.prompt: Build a clean, responsive pricing table with 3 tiers (Free, Pro, Enterprise) and a toggle between monthly/annual billing. Make it production-ready HTML.
evals.expected_output: A responsive pricing component with three tier cards, a billing toggle with smooth animation, clear feature comparison, and prominent CTAs

assertions.name: has-3-tiers
assertions.description: Three pricing tiers present

assertions.name: has-billing-toggle
assertions.description: Monthly/annual toggle exists

assertions.name: toggle-animates
assertions.description: Toggle has CSS transition

assertions.name: responsive-layout
assertions.description: Works on mobile

assertions.name: has-feature-list
assertions.description: Each tier lists features

assertions.name: has-cta-buttons
assertions.description: Each tier has a CTA button

evals.name: checkout-fix
evals.prompt: The checkout flow has a 40% drop-off rate. Redesign the checkout page with better UX, clearer error handling, and trust signals. Build it as a working HTML page.
evals.expected_output: A streamlined checkout page with inline validation, clear error messages, trust signals, order summary, and mobile-optimized layout

assertions.name: has-inline-validation
assertions.description: Form fields show validation states

assertions.name: has-error-messages
assertions.description: Specific, helpful error messages

assertions.name: has-trust-signals
assertions.description: Security badges or trust elements

assertions.name: has-order-summary
assertions.description: Visible order summary

assertions.name: responsive-layout
assertions.description: Mobile-responsive layout

assertions.name: has-progress-indicator
assertions.description: Checkout step indicator

evals.name: design-system-tokens
evals.prompt: /design-system Generate a complete token system from brand color #2563eb with dark mode support
evals.expected_output: CSS custom properties with full color palette (10 shades), semantic colors, typography scale, spacing scale, and dark mode overrides

assertions.name: has-color-shades

assertions.name: has-semantic-colors
assertions.description: Success, warning, error, info colors defined

assertions.name: has-dark-mode
assertions.description: Dark mode overrides present ([data-theme=dark] or @media prefers-color-scheme)

assertions.name: has-typography-scale
assertions.description: Font sizes from xs to 5xl defined

assertions.name: has-spacing-scale
assertions.description: Spacing values on a consistent grid

assertions.name: uses-css-variables
assertions.description: Output uses CSS custom properties

evals.name: design-review-audit
evals.prompt: /design-review ./evals/fixtures/test-page.html — audit this landing page for accessibility, usability, visual consistency, content quality, and motion design
evals.expected_output: Structured 5-point audit report with specific issues, severity levels, and actionable fix recommendations

assertions.name: checks-accessibility

assertions.name: checks-usability
assertions.description: Nielsen's heuristics referenced

assertions.name: checks-visual-consistency
assertions.description: Spacing, color, and typography consistency evaluated

assertions.name: has-severity-levels
assertions.description: Issues categorized by severity (critical, major, minor)

assertions.name: has-fix-suggestions
assertions.description: Each issue includes a specific remediation

assertions.name: has-summary-score
assertions.description: Overall quality score or assessment

evals.name: design-framework-react-tailwind
evals.prompt: /design-framework react-tailwind — Convert this landing page HTML to React + Tailwind components: a hero section with headline, subtext, and CTA button, plus a 3-column features grid
evals.expected_output: TypeScript React components with Tailwind classes, proper interface types, cn() utility, and component decomposition into Hero and FeatureGrid

assertions.name: has-typescript-interfaces
assertions.description: Props typed with TypeScript interface or type definitions

assertions.name: uses-tailwind-classes
assertions.description: Layout uses Tailwind utility classes, not inline styles or custom CSS

assertions.name: has-component-decomposition
assertions.description: Split into multiple components (e.g., Hero, FeatureCard, FeatureGrid)

assertions.name: has-cn-utility
assertions.description: Uses cn() or clsx() for conditional class merging

assertions.name: has-default-exports
assertions.description: Each component has a proper export

assertions.name: no-inline-styles
assertions.description: No style={{}} attributes — uses Tailwind classes instead

evals.name: design-framework-nextjs-app-router
evals.prompt: /design-framework nextjs — Convert this dashboard page HTML to Next.js App Router. The page has a static sidebar nav and a data table that fetches from an API endpoint.
evals.expected_output: Next.js App Router components with correct Server/Client component split — sidebar as Server Component, data table as Client Component with 'use client'

assertions.name: has-server-component
assertions.description: Sidebar or static sections are Server Components (no 'use client')

assertions.name: has-client-directive
assertions.description: Interactive components have 'use client' directive at top of file

assertions.name: has-async-data-fetch
assertions.description: Server component uses async/await for data fetching, not useEffect

assertions.name: has-typescript
assertions.description: Components use TypeScript with proper return type annotations

assertions.name: has-layout-structure
assertions.description: Uses layout.tsx and page.tsx pattern correctly

assertions.name: has-component-separation
assertions.description: Server and Client components are in separate files

evals.name: edge-case-dark-mode
evals.prompt: Build a notification settings page with full dark mode support — the page should look polished in both light and dark themes without any broken contrast or invisible elements
evals.expected_output: Settings page with toggle switches, sections, and descriptions that passes WCAG AA contrast in both light and dark modes, using prefers-color-scheme

assertions.name: has-prefers-color-scheme
assertions.description: @media (prefers-color-scheme: dark) is used for dark mode

assertions.name: dark-bg-defined
assertions.description: Dark mode background color explicitly overrides light mode background

assertions.name: dark-text-defined
assertions.description: Dark mode text color explicitly defined (not invisible on dark bg)

assertions.name: dark-border-defined
assertions.description: Border colors adjusted for dark mode (not invisible)

assertions.name: has-toggle-switches
assertions.description: Toggle/checkbox inputs with CSS-styled visual states

assertions.name: has-interactive-states
assertions.description: :hover and :focus states work in both light and dark modes

evals.name: edge-case-rtl-layout
evals.prompt: Design a product card component that must work correctly in both LTR (English) and RTL (Arabic/Hebrew) layouts. Use CSS logical properties.
evals.expected_output: Product card using CSS logical properties (margin-inline-start, padding-inline-end, etc.) and dir='rtl' support, so text and layout flip correctly

assertions.name: uses-logical-properties
assertions.description: CSS uses margin-inline-start/end or padding-inline-start/end instead of left/right

assertions.name: has-dir-rtl-demo
assertions.description: HTML includes dir='rtl' demo or test showing RTL layout

assertions.name: text-aligns-correctly
assertions.description: Text alignment uses 'start'/'end' not 'left'/'right'

assertions.name: icons-flip-in-rtl
assertions.description: Directional icons (arrows) use transform: scaleX(-1) or CSS logical values in RTL

assertions.name: no-absolute-positioning-breaks
assertions.description: Layout doesn't break with dir='rtl' applied to container

assertions.name: has-rtl-comment
assertions.description: Code includes comment or note explaining the RTL approach used

evals.name: edge-case-reduced-motion
evals.prompt: Build an animated hero section with entrance animations, a rotating logo, and a typing effect — but ensure all animations respect prefers-reduced-motion.
evals.expected_output: Hero with CSS animations that are disabled or reduced when prefers-reduced-motion: reduce is detected, without breaking the layout or removing content

assertions.name: has-animations
assertions.description: CSS animations or transitions present on hero elements

assertions.name: has-reduced-motion-query
assertions.description: @media (prefers-reduced-motion: reduce) media query present

assertions.name: animations-disabled-in-query
assertions.description: animation: none or transition: none applied inside reduced-motion query

assertions.name: layout-intact-without-animation
assertions.description: Content is still visible and accessible when animations are disabled

assertions.name: typing-effect-handled
assertions.description: Typing animation shows final text immediately when reduced-motion is preferred

assertions.name: no-autoplaying-video
assertions.description: Any motion content (video/gif) has controls or is paused by default

evals.name: workflow-design-to-framework

evals.expected_output: First: complete HTML pricing page. Then: React TypeScript components for PricingPage, PricingCard, and BillingToggle with Tailwind classes and proper prop types

assertions.name: has-react-components

assertions.name: has-billing-toggle-component

assertions.name: has-pricing-card-props
assertions.description: PricingCard accepts plan name, price, features array as typed props

assertions.name: tailwind-for-layout
assertions.description: Grid and flex layout uses Tailwind classes (grid-cols-3, flex, gap-)

assertions.name: state-management-correct
assertions.description: Toggle state uses React useState, not a global store or context

evals.name: workflow-brand-to-component-docs
evals.prompt: Start with /brand-kit #f59e0b warm, then use /design to build a button component system based on those brand tokens, then run /design-review to audit the result.
evals.expected_output: Brand kit tokens → Button component system → Design review report. Each phase's output feeds the next.

assertions.name: brand-kit-tokens-present
assertions.description: CSS custom properties or token definitions derived from #f59e0b

assertions.name: button-uses-brand-tokens
assertions.description: Button component references the brand token variables, not hardcoded colors

assertions.name: has-button-variants

assertions.name: review-references-brand
assertions.description: Design review audit checks token usage and brand consistency

assertions.name: review-checks-accessibility
assertions.description: Review includes contrast ratio check for button text on brand background

assertions.name: workflow-connected
assertions.description: Each phase explicitly references or uses output from the previous phase

evals.name: workflow-email-social-launch
evals.prompt: Plan a product launch for a new AI writing tool. First use /email-campaign to build a 3-email launch sequence, then /social-campaign for a 2-week social push on LinkedIn and Twitter.
evals.expected_output: Coordinated launch plan: email sequence (teaser, launch, follow-up) + social campaign with unified messaging across both channels

assertions.name: has-3-launch-emails
assertions.description: Email sequence has teaser, launch day, and follow-up emails

assertions.name: email-subject-lines-written
assertions.description: All 3 email subject lines and preview texts are written

assertions.name: has-social-campaign-plan
assertions.description: Social campaign includes platform-specific content calendar

assertions.name: messaging-aligned
assertions.description: Email and social copy share consistent key messages and value props

assertions.name: has-launch-timing
assertions.description: Both email and social plans reference a coordinated launch date/window

assertions.name: has-cta-consistency
assertions.description: Primary CTA (e.g., 'Try free') is consistent across email and social

evals.name: depth-ui-component-states
evals.prompt: /design Build a quantity selector component for an e-commerce product page.
evals.expected_output: Component design that explicitly addresses all 8 states: default, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error, and empty — not just the happy-path default state

assertions.name: has-all-8-states
assertions.description: Design explicitly covers default, hover, focus, active, disabled, loading, error, and empty/skeleton states

assertions.name: has-disabled-treatment
assertions.description: Disabled state specifies visual treatment (opacity or muted color) and cursor behavior

assertions.name: has-error-treatment
assertions.description: Error state includes visual indicator plus descriptive text, not just color

assertions.name: has-loading-state
assertions.description: Loading state described for async operations

assertions.name: accessibility-in-focus-state
assertions.description: Focus state specifies an outline (not just background change) for keyboard navigation

evals.name: depth-ux-empty-states
evals.prompt: /design Design the empty states for a project management dashboard that shows tasks, activity feed, and team members.
evals.expected_output: Distinct empty state designs for all 4 types: first-use, no results, error, and offline — each with appropriate illustration/icon, headline, explanation, and CTA

assertions.name: covers-first-use-empty-state
assertions.description: First-use empty state has headline + explanation + primary CTA

assertions.name: covers-no-results-empty-state
assertions.description: No-results state acknowledges the search/filter and offers an escape

assertions.name: covers-error-empty-state
assertions.description: Error state uses plain language with a retry action

assertions.name: covers-offline-empty-state
assertions.description: Offline state distinguishes itself from error and indicates what's still available

assertions.name: ctaS-are-specific
assertions.description: Each empty state CTA is a specific verb+object, not generic 'Try again' or 'OK'

evals.name: depth-product-jtbd
evals.prompt: /design We want to add a 'saved searches' feature to our job board. Plan the design.
evals.expected_output: Design plan that applies JTBD thinking — identifies the job being done, the functional/emotional/social dimensions, and uses this to shape what actually gets built

assertions.name: identifies-the-job
assertions.description: Articulates the job-to-be-done in 'when / motivation / outcome' format, not just the feature description

assertions.name: covers-emotional-dimension
assertions.description: Identifies an emotional or social dimension of the job beyond the functional task

assertions.name: uses-jtbd-to-scope
assertions.description: JTBD analysis leads to a scoping decision — what to include and what to exclude based on the job

assertions.name: defines-success-metric
assertions.description: Proposes a measurable success metric tied to the job, not just feature adoption

assertions.name: has-assumption-list
assertions.description: Lists at least 2 assumptions that must be true for the feature to succeed

evals.name: depth-content-error-messages
evals.prompt: /design Write the error messages for a file upload component that handles: file too large, wrong format, upload failed, and virus detected.
evals.expected_output: Error messages following the 3-part formula (what happened + why + what to do) in plain language with no technical jargon or user-blaming

assertions.name: no-jargon-in-errors
assertions.description: No technical terms (error codes, HTTP status, internal names) in any error message

assertions.name: no-user-blame
assertions.description: No passive-aggressive or blaming language ('You uploaded an invalid file' → 'That file format isn't supported')

assertions.name: all-4-errors-addressed
assertions.description: All 4 error types have distinct, specific messages

assertions.name: each-has-recovery-action
assertions.description: Every error message includes a specific action the user can take to resolve it

assertions.name: plain-language-throughout
assertions.description: All messages are readable by a non-technical user at a glance

evals.name: depth-dataviz-chart-selection
evals.prompt: /design A SaaS analytics dashboard needs to show: monthly revenue trend, plan distribution (free/pro/agency), feature adoption rates across 8 features, and correlation between usage frequency and retention. Which chart types for each and why?
evals.expected_output: Chart type recommendations with explicit rationale referencing the data relationship (comparison/distribution/composition/relationship) — not just naming the chart type

assertions.name: uses-data-relationship-framework
assertions.description: Recommendation references the data relationship (comparison, distribution, composition, or relationship) for each choice

assertions.name: appropriate-chart-for-trend
assertions.description: Monthly revenue trend uses a line chart (not bar) with justification

assertions.name: appropriate-chart-for-distribution
assertions.description: Plan distribution recommendation addresses the pie/donut question explicitly with a reasoned choice

assertions.name: feature-adoption-handles-many-series
assertions.description: 8-feature adoption recommendation accounts for the many-series problem (small multiples or grouped bar, not a 8-line chart)

assertions.name: correlation-uses-scatter
assertions.description: Usage-retention correlation uses scatter plot with rationale

evals.name: depth-ux-research-synthesis
evals.prompt: /ux-audit We ran 6 usability tests on our onboarding flow. 4 of 6 participants got confused at step 3 (connecting their first integration). How should we synthesise and present these findings to get the team to act?
evals.expected_output: Synthesis approach using insight-implication-recommendation structure, with specific guidance on how to present findings that lead to a design decision — not just a report

assertions.name: uses-insight-implication-recommendation
assertions.description: Response structures findings as: insight (what happened) → implication (what it means) → recommendation (what to do)

assertions.name: quantifies-the-severity
assertions.description: Uses the 4/6 finding to frame severity — not just 'some users were confused'

assertions.name: gives-specific-recommendation
assertions.description: Recommendation is a specific design change, not 'improve step 3'

assertions.name: addresses-how-to-present
assertions.description: Gives guidance on how to frame findings for a decision-maker audience

assertions.name: notes-what-more-research-would-add
assertions.description: Acknowledges what quant data would complement the qual finding

evals.name: depth-figma-token-architecture
evals.prompt: /figma Our design team is building a multi-brand design system for 3 clients in Figma. How should we structure the variable/token architecture?
evals.expected_output: Three-tier token architecture (primitive → semantic → component) with a specific multi-brand structure — not a generic 'use tokens' recommendation

assertions.name: uses-three-tier-hierarchy
assertions.description: Response describes primitive tokens, semantic tokens, and optionally component tokens as distinct tiers

assertions.name: explains-brand-swap-at-semantic-layer
assertions.description: Multi-brand structure swaps at the semantic layer (not the primitive layer), preserving the primitive foundation

assertions.name: gives-naming-examples
assertions.description: Provides concrete token naming examples at each tier

assertions.name: addresses-scope-decision
assertions.description: Addresses whether tokens should be local or published to the shared library

assertions.name: handles-auto-update
assertions.description: Explains how component tokens inherit from semantic tokens so brand switches propagate automatically

evals.name: depth-deployment-core-web-vitals
evals.prompt: /design Our Next.js app has an LCP of 4.2s and a CLS score of 0.18. What are the most likely causes and how do we fix them?
evals.expected_output: Specific, actionable diagnosis and fixes for both LCP and CLS — not generic 'optimise images' advice

assertions.name: diagnoses-lcp-causes-specifically
assertions.description: Identifies specific LCP causes (unpreloaded hero image, render-blocking resources, no SSR) not just 'large images'

assertions.name: provides-lcp-code-fix
assertions.description: Includes a concrete code fix for at least one LCP cause (preload link tag, next/image config, etc.)

assertions.name: diagnoses-cls-causes-specifically
assertions.description: Identifies specific CLS causes (missing image dimensions, font swap, injected content) not just 'layout shifts'

assertions.name: provides-cls-code-fix
assertions.description: Includes a concrete code fix for at least one CLS cause (width/height attributes, font-display, reserved space)

assertions.name: references-measurement-tool
assertions.description: Mentions how to measure/verify the fix (Lighthouse, CrUX, PerformanceObserver)

evals.name: depth-email-subject-lines
evals.prompt: /email-template Write 5 subject line variants for a re-engagement email to users who haven't logged in for 30 days.
evals.expected_output: 5 subject lines using different formulas (specificity, curiosity, direct benefit, etc.) — not 5 variations of the same approach

assertions.name: uses-multiple-formulas
assertions.description: At least 3 distinct subject line formulas used across the 5 variants

assertions.name: includes-preview-text-guidance
assertions.description: Provides corresponding preview text for at least 2 variants

assertions.name: no-anti-patterns
assertions.description: No ALL CAPS, no clickbait, no excessive punctuation, no misleading Re:/Fwd: tricks

assertions.name: variants-are-meaningfully-different
assertions.description: Variants test different approaches (benefit vs. curiosity vs. urgency), not just different wording of the same idea

assertions.name: respects-character-limit
assertions.description: All subject lines are under 50 characters for mobile preview

evals.name: depth-social-platform-constraints
evals.prompt: /social-content Design a campaign announcement post for a product launch that needs to work across Instagram feed, LinkedIn, and TikTok.
evals.expected_output: Platform-specific adaptations that respect each platform's safe zones, aspect ratios, and tone — not the same post resized

assertions.name: specifies-dimensions-per-platform
assertions.description: Gives correct dimensions or aspect ratios for each platform (Instagram 4:5, LinkedIn 1200x628 or square, TikTok 9:16)

assertions.name: addresses-safe-zones
assertions.description: Mentions safe zone constraints for at least one platform (especially TikTok/Reels)

assertions.name: adapts-tone-per-platform
assertions.description: Copy/tone is adapted per platform, not identical across all three

assertions.name: uses-fixed-flexible-adaptive-model
assertions.description: Identifies which elements stay fixed (logo, colours) and which adapt (layout, copy length)

assertions.name: no-copy-paste-approach
assertions.description: Response explicitly addresses why the same asset can't be used across all platforms
